ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for roasted turkey with skin; FoodOn and FoodEx2 anchors are sufficient for this record.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of roasted turkey with skin is approximately 85 g (about 3 oz), equivalent to two to three slices of breast or thigh meat with skin attached.

nutrient_source.title: Turkey, all classes, meat and skin, cooked, roasted — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB No. 05165)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for roasted turkey, all classes, meat and skin, is an exact match for this food record. Singapore HPB values (energy 144 kcal, protein 21.64 g, fat 5.64 g, carbohydrate 0.14 g) are consistent with USDA SR Legacy values and are used as the primary per-100g basis. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and additional micronutrients are supplemented from USDA SR Legacy where HPB does not report them.

gi_source.title: N/A — Roasted turkey with skin contains negligible carbohydrates (0.14 g/100 g); glycemic index is not applicable for this food
gi_source.source_match_notes: Roasted turkey with skin contains only 0.14 g carbohydrate per 100 g. Glycemic index testing is not performed on foods with negligible carbohydrate content. A numeric sentinel value of 0 is used here solely to satisfy the requirement that glycemic_index be numeric; gi_category is set to 'not_applicable' and GL per serving is 0.0 to reflect that no meaningful glycaemic response occurs. No GI value is assigned from any GI database.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium (112 mg/100 g) with a favourable sodium-to-potassium ratio (112 mg Na : 224 mg K)
hypertension.guidance: Roasted turkey with skin is appropriate for hypertension management given its low sodium content of 112 mg/100 g and a potassium level of 224 mg/100 g; individuals should avoid pre-basted, pre-seasoned, or brined commercial turkey products that may contain substantially higher sodium.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Negligible carbohydrate (0.14 g/100 g) and high protein (21.64 g/100 g) supporting glycaemic stability
type2_diabetes.guidance: With only 0.14 g carbohydrate per 100 g and 21.64 g protein, roasted turkey with skin has a negligible glycaemic impact and supports satiety and lean muscle maintenance in individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; glazed or honey-roasted preparations should be avoided as they add significant sugar.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate total fat (5.64 g/100 g) with saturated fat at 1.55 g/100 g and zero trans fat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Roasted turkey with skin contains a moderate fat profile with saturated fat at 1.55 g/100 g and no trans fat, making it acceptable within a heart-healthy diet; removing the skin reduces saturated fat further to approximately 0.9 g/100 g for individuals requiring stricter lipid management.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Absence of positive anti-inflammatory nutrients; saturated fat within acceptable limits
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Roasted turkey with skin is not contraindicated in rheumatoid arthritis given its low saturated fat (1.55 g/100 g) and zero trans fat, but it provides no positive anti-inflammatory benefit (no omega-3, antioxidants, or polyphenols); removing the skin and pairing with anti-inflammatory foods such as oily fish or cruciferous vegetables is recommended.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Roasted turkey with skin is a whole or portioned turkey that has been oven-roasted until fully cooked, retaining its skin throughout the cooking process. The result is a light to golden-brown exterior with a tender, juicy interior across breast, thigh, drumstick, and wing cuts. The skin contributes additional fat and a slightly crisp texture compared to skinless preparations. Nutritionally, roasted turkey with skin is a high-protein food providing 21.64 g protein per 100 g, with a moderate total fat content of 5.64 g (saturated fat approximately 1.55 g) and negligible carbohydrate at 0.14 g. Energy density is 144 kcal per 100 g. It is a meaningful source of B vitamins including niacin (6.34 mg), B6 (0.53 mg), and B12 (0.35 mcg), as well as selenium (26.5 mcg), zinc (2.27 mg), and phosphorus (185 mg). Sodium is low at 112 mg/100 g, making it suitable for sodium-conscious diets when prepared without added salt or basting solutions. Clinically, this food is appropriate for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes due to its negligible glycaemic impact, and for hypertension due to its low sodium and favourable potassium ratio. It is broadly suitable for cardiovascular health within a balanced diet. Turkey is widely consumed in North American and Western culinary traditions, particularly associated with festive occasions such as Thanksgiving and Christmas, but is also consumed year-round as a lean protein source.

Additional micronutrient values cross-referenced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy. Both sources are approved primary sources under Project Veritas v2 source policy.
notes: Dietary fibre is 0.0 g/100 g: confirmed zero per HPB and USDA SR Legacy — turkey meat contains no dietary fibre. Trans fat is 0.0 g/100 g: confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy for roasted turkey. Vitamin C is 0.0 mg/100 g and Vitamin A is 0.0 mcg RAE: confirmed negligible per USDA SR Legacy for roasted turkey meat and skin. Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates (0.14 g/100 g); gi_category is set to 'not_applicable'. A numeric value of 0 is stored in the glycemic_index field to satisfy the validator requirement that glycemic_index be numeric; this does not represent a tested GI value. Glycemic load per serving is 0.0. Saturated fat (1.55 g/100 g), monounsaturated_fat_g (1.87 g/100 g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (1.43 g/100 g), and micronutrient values not reported by HPB are supplemented from USDA SR Legacy (NDB No. 05165). The rheumatoid_arthritis suitability flag from the input has been preserved in additional_conditions with corrected concern level (moderate rather than unsuitable, as the food is not contraindicated but lacks positive anti-inflammatory factors). No multilingual local names are required as this is a Western food item, not a Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, or Vietnamese dish.
